title: L'Investitore Intelligente
author: Benjamin Graham
quotes: L'investitore intelligente e' un realista che vende agli ottimisti e compra dai pessimisti.

Con disciplina e coraggio, potete evitare che gli sbalzi di umore altrui influenzino il vostro destino finanziario. In definitiva, il comportamento dei titolo in cui investite e' molto meno importante del vostro.
Le perdite davvero terribili si verificano sempre quando l'investitore si dimentica di chiedere "Quanto?".
Il problema principale dell'investitore - e forse anche il peggior nemico - e' probabilmente lui stesso.

Quando tutti decidono che un certo settore e' palesemente il migliore in cui investitore, il prezzo dei suoi titoli e' ormai cresciuto cosi a dismisura che i rendimenti futuri non potranno che calare.
L'investitore intelligente si rende conto che i titoli diventano piu' rischiosi, non meno, all'aumentare del loro prezzo; e meno rischiosi, non piu', al calare dei prezzi.
Il futuro dei prezzi degli strumenti finanziari non e' mai prevedibile.
Gli americani stanno diventando piu' forti. Vent'anni fa servivano due persone per portare10 dollari di spesa dal supermercato a casa; oggi ci riesce anche un bambino di cinque anni.
L'unica domanda da non fare mai a un burocrate e' "Perche'?"
Per accumulare una fortuna servono grande audacia e grande cautela; e una volta accumulata, serve dieci volte piu' intelligenza per conservarla.
Il motivo per cui investiamo non e' guadagnare piu' soldi della media, ma guadagnare abbastanza per soddisfare le nostre esigenze
L'investimento piu' intelligente e' quello gestito come fosse un'azienda
Nel mondo dei titoli, il coraggio diventa la virtu' suprema dopo aver raggiunto un livello di conoscenza adeguato e aver messo alla prova la propria capacita' di giudizio
E' piu' facile di quanto si pensi ottenere risultati soddisfacenti da un investimento; ottenere risultati superiori e' piu' difficile di come sembri
concepts: Cosa significa essere un investitore intelligente? Significa essere pazienti, disciplinati e desiderosi di imparare, oltre a saper controllare le emozioni e pensare con la propria testa. Questo tipo di intelligenza e' piu' una questione di carattere che di cervello.

Un'operazione di investimento e' un'attivita' che, dopo un'analisi approfondita, promette la sicurezza del capitale e un rendimento adeguato. Le operazioni che non soddisfano questi requisiti sono speculative.
Mai mescolare le operazioni speculative e di investimento nello stesso conto, ne' in nessuna area del vostro pensiero.

L'investitore difensivo deve limitarsi alle azioni di aziende importanti con una lunga storia di profitti e in condizioni finanziarie solide. Gli investitori aggressivi possono comprare altri tipi di azioni ordinarie, ma farebbero bene a limitarsi a quelle che risultino piu' attraenti in seguito a un'analisi intelligente.
Mai allocare piu' del 10 % del proprio portafoglio alla spazio dedicato ai cosiddetti "colpi di testa", cioe' quella parte di capitale che si vuole dedicare alla pura speculazione sull'azionario.

Il **Dividend Yeld** e' il rapporto tra il dividendo in contanti e il prezzo di un'azione. Se un'azienda paga un dividendo annuo di 2 dollari per azione quando il prezzo dell'azione e' di 100 dollari, il suo Yeld e' pari al 2%. Percio' il Dividend Yeld di un'azione e' fortemente influenzato dal prezzo dell'azione

L'investitore deve imporre un limite di prezzo che e' disposto a pagare per un'azione in relazione ai suoi utili medi negli ultimi sette anni circa. Il suggerimento e' di porre questo limite a 25 volte gli utili medi (per avere un calcolo veritiero, basarsi sugli utili degli ultimi 6 anni e fare una media).

"Mai comprare una causa legale". Per l'investitore e' troppo rischioso comprare dei titoli azionari dopo una brusca discesa del prezzo a causa di accuse o cause legali in corso. Nella maggior parte dei casi queste situazioni portano a reali svalutazioni del titolo andando quindi a portare gravi perdite nel portafoglio dell'investitore. 
Un esempio contrario e' quello di Philip Morris che perse il 23% in 2 giorni quando ammise che le sigarette provocavano tumori, ma poi nel giro di un solo anno il titolo ando' a raddoppiare il proprio valore.
Il mercato azionario spesso si sbaglia di grosso, e a volte un investitore dotato di prontezza e coraggio puo' trarre vantaggio dai suoi errori palesi.

Quasi tutte le aziende cambiano carattere e qualita' nel corso degli anni, a volte in meglio, piu' spesso in peggio. L'investitore non deve seguire con occhio di falco i risultati delle sue aziende; ma e' giusto che di tanto in tanto le osservi con attenzione.

Quali fattori determinano la cifra che dovresti pagare per un titolo? Si possono riassumere in questi 5 punti.
1. Le prospettive generali a lungo termine dell'azienda
2. La qualità della sua dirigenza
3. La sua forza economica e la struttura del capitale
4. Lo storico dei suoi dividendi (20 anni di pagamenti regolari)
5. Il pay out

Oggi l'investitore intelligente dovrebbe scaricare almeno 5 relazioni annuali dal sito dell'azienda, poi leggere attentamente i documenti finanziari, raccogliendo informazioni per rispondere a queste domande:
1. Cosa fa crescere questa azienda?
2. Da dove vengono i suoi profitti?
Analizzare la struttura del capitale dell'azienda. Leggete il bilancio per vedere quanto debito ha l'azienda; in generale, il debito a lungo termine dovrebbe essere sotto il 50% del capitale totale.

L'azienda deve dimostrare che conviene se non paga i dividendi agli azionisti. Se l'azienda ha costantemente sovraperformato la concorrenza nei mercati buoni e in quelli cattivi, i dirigenti stanno evidentemente usando bene la liquidità che hanno a disposizione.

Le aziende che frazionano ripetutamente le azioni, e se ne vantano nei comunicati stampa, mostrano di prendere per stupidi i loro investitori. 2 azioni di un titolo a 50 dollari non valgono più di un'azione sola a 100 dollari.
Le aziende dovrebbero ricomprare le loro azioni quando costano poco, non quando toccano i massimi storici.

I 7 criteri quantitativi e qualitativi da applicare nella selezione di azioni ordinarie:
1. Adeguate dimensione dell'impresa - Non meno di 100 milioni di fatturato annuo
2. Una situazione finanziaria sufficientemente solida - Le attivita' correnti devono essere almeno il doppio delle passivita' correnti
3. Stabilita' degli utili - Le azioni ordinarie devono aver generato un utile in ciascuno degli ultimi 10 anni
4. Regolarita' dei dividendi - Pagamenti ininterrotti, almeno per gli ultimi 20 anni
5. Crescita degli utili - Un incremento minimo di almeno un terzo degli utili per azione negli ultimi 10 anni
6. Moderato rapporto prezzo/utili - Il prezzo corrente non deve essere superiore a 15 volte gli utili medi degli ultimi 3 anni
7. Moderato rapporto prezzo/asset - Il prezzo corrente non deve superare una volta e mezzo il book value dichiarato. Come regola generale il prodotto del moltiplicatore per il rapporto prezzo/book value non sia superiore a 22,5

Se si possiede una obbligazione convertibile, si possiede anche un'opzione: si puo' conservare l'obbligazione e continuare a guadagnarne gli interessi, oppure si puo' scambiarla con azioni ordinarie della societa' emittente a un rapporto predeterminato.
Poiche' sono scambiabili con azioni, le convertibili pagano interessi piu' bassi rispetto alla maggior parte delle obbligazioni comparabili.

Le 4 regole dell'investitore intelligente:
1. Conoscere il proprio mestiere e assicurarsi di sapere quel che uno fa
2. Non fate gestire il vostro business a nessun altro, a meno che possiate supervisionare la sua performance con adeguata cura e competenza o abbiate motivi insolitamente validi per fidarvi implicitamente della sua integrita' e capacita'
3. Non entrate in un'operazione se un calcola affidabile non vi ha mostrato che ha buone probabilita' di generare un profitto ragionevole.
4. Abbiate fiducia nella vostra conoscenza ed esperienza

title: Start With Why
author: Simon Sinek
quotes: Thera only two ways to influence human behavior: you can manipulate it or you can inspire it.

WHY: Very few people or companies can clearly articulate why they do what they do. When I say why, I don't mean to make money - that's a result-. By why I mean what is your purpose, cause or belief? Why does your company exist? Why do you get out of bed every morning? And why should anyone care?
People don't buy WHAT you do, they buy WHY you do iy

The goal of business should not be to do business with anyone who simply wants what you have. It should be to focus on the people who believe what you believe. When we are selective about doing business only those believe in our WHY l, trust emerges.

concepts:
Alcune tecniche di manipolazione o ispirazione:
1. Price —> Il gioco del prezzo va usato con molta cautela da parte delle aziende, in quanto il risultato di breve periodo puo' sembrare ottimo, ma il taglio del prezzo e di conseguenza dei profitti, nel lungo termine porta a pessimi risultati
2. Promotions —> Anche le promozioni sono molto pericolose nel lungo termine, andando anche in questo caso a erodere i profitti, ma sopratutto abituando i clienti ad aspettarsele, cosi che smettano di comprare non appena le promozioni vengono tolte. Una buona tecnica di promozione e' quella del rimborso per insoddisfazione, in quanto solo una piccola percentuale dei clienti porteranno avanti la complicata procedura per ottenere il rimborso, restituendo l'articolo
3. Fear —> La paura viene spesso usata come ottima strategia di vendita in quanto permette un alto tasso di manipolazione del cliente. Es "In media una casa su 2 ha qualche infestazione, chiama subito per avere un controllo della tua abitazione"
4. Aspiration —> Creare aspirazione nel raggiungimento del risultato finale porta a grandi risultati di vendita. E' necessario creare nel cliente l'idea di poter raggiungere grandi risultati in breve tempo
5. Peer Pressure —> Far credere ai clienti che il prodotto o servizio abbia gia' manifestato successo in molte altre persone, cosi da creare la sensazione di poter ottenere lo stesso avendo una controprova cosi ampia. Questa tecnica funziona perche' crediamo che la maggioranza degli esperti ne sappia molto piu' di noi, percio' tendiamo a fidarci ciecamente
6. Innovation —>

Le grandi aziende, nelle strategie pubblicitarie partono dal "Perche'"; in pratica non cercano di spiegare in partenza perche' i loro prodotti sono belli, ma iniziano a spiegare al pubblico il perche'  la loro filosofia e il loro scopo con azienda sia innovativo, nobile o altro, usando il "Cosa" come prova tangibile della strategia del perche'.

Tutte le grandi personalità imprenditoriali (WHY People), per creare quello che hanno creato, avevano al loro fianco una persona con le competenze e capacità per mettere in pratica le idee (HOW People). I più grandi esempi sono Jobs e Wozniack, Gates e Allen, Walt DIsney e Roy Disney

title: The Bitcoin Standard

title: Scrum: The Art of Doing Twice the Work in Half the Time
author: Jeff Sutherland
quotes: **Planning is useful. Blindly following plans is stupid**
**Inspect and adapt**. Every little while, stop doing what you're doing, review what you've done, and see if it's still what you should be doing and if you can do it better
**Change or die**. Clinging to the old way of doing things, of command and control and rigid predictability, will bring only failure.
**Fail fast so you can fix early**
Whenever there are handoffs between teams, there is the opportunity for disaster
The sooner you give things to your customers, the quicker they can tell you if you're making something they need
Don't fall in love with you plan. It's almost certainly wrong
concepts: Scrum works by setting sequential goal that must be completed in a fixed length of time

PDCA cycle — **P**lan, **D**o, **C**heck, **A**ct

> **Planning is useful. Blindly following plans is stupid**
>

> **Inspect and adapt**. Every little while, stop doing what you're doing, review what you've done, and see if it's still what you should be doing and if you can do it better
>

> **Change or die**. Clinging to the old way of doing things, of command and control and rigid predictability, will bring only failure.
>

> **Fail fast so you can fix early**
>

> Whenever there are handoffs between teams, there is the opportunity for disaster
>

> The sooner you give things to your customers, the quicker they can tell you if you're making something they need
>

> Don't fall in love with you plan. It's almost certainly wrong
>
nozioni:
**How to Begin SCRUM**
Pick a Product Owner - This person is the one with vision of what you are going to do, make, or accomplish.
Pick a Team - This team needs to have all the skills needed to take the Product Owner' vision and make it a reality
Pick a Scrum Master -  This is the person who will coach the rest of the team through the Scrum framework and help the team eliminate anything that is slowing them down

Create and prioritize a Product Backlog - This is a list at a high level of everything that needs to be built or done to make that vision a reality. At any point, the Product Backlog is the single, definitive view of "everything that could be done by the team ever, in order of priority"
Refine and Estimate the Product Backlog - It is crucial that the people who are actually going to complete the items in the Product Backlog estimate how much effort they will take
Sprint Planning - This is the first of the Scrum meetings. Sprints are always a fixed length of time that is less than a month.
Make Work Visible - Create a Scrum Board  with 3 columns: To Do, Doing, Done
Daily Stand-up - Each day, at the same time, for no more than 15 minutes, the team and the Scrum Master meet and answer three questions:
What did you do yesterday to help the team finish the Sprint?
What will you do today to help the team finish the Sprint?
Is there any obstacle blocking you or the team from achieving the Sprint Goal?
Sprint Review - This is the meeting where the teams shows what they are accomplished during the Sprint

Sprint Retrospective - After the team has shown what they have accomplished during the last Sprint, they sit down and think about what went right, what could have gone better and what can be made better in the next Sprint
Immediately start the next Sprint Cycle

To be frugal means to have a high joy-to-stuff-ratio. If you get one unit of joy for each material possession, that's frugal. But if you need ten possessions to even begin registering on the joy meter, you are missing the point of being alive
nozioni:
**Commento Personale**
Ho trovato il libro a tratti molto interessante e piacevole nella lettura. Valido nel far capire e intendere quale debba essere il reale valore da attribuire ai soldi rispetto alle esperienze di vita vissuta.
In altre parti ho trovato il libro un pò noioso con momenti di facile distrazione

title: Indistractable: How to Control Your Attention and Choose Your Life
author: Nir Eyal
quotes: Being indistractable means to do what you say you will do. Indistractable people are as honest with themselves as they are with others
**Time management is pain management**. Distractions cost us time, and, like all actions, they are spurred by the desire to escape discomfort
What we fear doing most is usually what we most need to do
nozioni:
**Commento personale**
Libro molto interessante e capace di far rendere conto di quello che non si riesce a volte a realizzare in autonomia. 
Valide riflessioni e studi riportati sull'impatto della tecnologia sulle nostre vite e tanti dati sull'importanza di crearsi una scaletta da seguire nell'organizzazione delle proprie giornate per ottenere il massimo da ogni task.
Non do 5 stelle per alcune parti del libro che mi sono parse leggermente banali

title: La Fattoria degli Animali
author: George Orwell
nozioni:
**Commento Personale**
Libro fantastico per il modo in cui sotto forma di fiaba riesce a creare una metafora ideale per rappresentare quella che è la nostra attuale società, in cui la fetta di persone che "governa" ha il potere di plasmare la realtà a proprio piacimento cosi che il "popolo" creda a qualsiasi cosa

title: How to Be Everything: A Guide for Those Who (Still) Don't Know What They Want to Be When They Grow Up
author: Emilie Wapnick
quotes: Multipotentialites tend to struggle with three main areas: work, productivity and self-esteem.

Multipotentialites often invite to much variety into our lives by overbooking ourselves. We have an intense Desire to learn and experience new things. We may very well be able to pursue all these things (and more) over the course of our Life, but pursuing them all at once could overwhelm us to the point where we aren't having much fun.
The Einstein Approach is having one full-time job or business that fully supports you, while leaving you with enough time and energy to pursue your other passions on the side.
To most people, finishing means hitting an external end point. Multipotentialities, on the other hand, are finished once we get what we came for.
We overestimate what we can accomplish in a day, but underestimate what we can accomplish in a year.
nozioni:
**Commento Personale**
Libro utile per rendermi conto di quale sia la mia attitudine nell'affrontare il lavoro, lo sport e più in generale tutti gli aspetti della mia vita.

Libro che rileggerei una seconda volta con una maggiore padronanza della lingua inglese per poter cogliere ancora più particolari utili

title: Go Live!: Turn Virtual Connections into Paying Customers
author: Jeffrey Gitomer
quotes: The CANDY BAR Method:
Stop focusing on yourself and start focusing on your customer
HELP CUSTOMERS WIN AND YOU WIN. You win their hearts and minds first, and eventually, when their time is right, you will win the order
Make a game plan to HELP before you make a game plan to SELL
If you can make them laugh, you can make them buy
nozioni:
**Commento Personale**
Lettura davvero scorrevole e ottimi spunti di riflessione e input su cosa concentrarsi per ottenere l'attenzione del pubblico a cui si vuole vendere.
Libro incentrato in buona parte sulla vendita tramite video e quindi lontana dal mio interesse per ecommerce

title: Thinking, Fast and Slow
author: Daniel Kahneman
quotes: We can blind to the obvious, and we are also blind to our blindness
It is easier to recognize other people's mistakes than our own
Intelligence is not only the ability to reason; it is also the ability to find relevant material in memory and to deploy attention when needed.
You know far less about yourself than you feel to know
His System 1 constructed a story, and his System 2 believed it. It happens to all of us.
If you can't solve a problem, then there is an easier problem you can solve: find it!
Our thoughts and our behaviors are influenced, much more than we know or want, by environment of the moment.
Because of the coincidence of two planes crashing last month, she now prefers to take the train. That's silly. The risk hasn't really changed: it's an availability bias.
The core of the illusion is that we believe we understand the past, which implies that the future also should be knowable, but in fact we understand the past less than we believe we do.
What we learn from the past is to maximize the qualities of our future memories, not necessarily of our future experience.
Nothing in life is as important as you think it is when you are thinking about it
concepts:
There are 2 systems in the mind:
1- System 1 operates automatically and quickly, with little or no effort and no sense of voluntary control. It distinguishes the surprising from the normal.
2- System 2 allocates attention to the effortful mental activities that demand it, including complex computations. The operations of system 2 are often associated with the subjective experience of agency, choice and concentration
Most of what you (your system 2) think and do originates in your system 1, but system 2 takes over when things get difficult, and it normally has the last word.
The state of effortless attending is named flow and is "a state of effortless concentration so deep that you lose your sense of time, of yourselves , of your problems"
Activities that impose high demands on System 2 require self-control, and the exertion of self-control is depleting and unpleasant.

Ego Depletion is the phenomenon that an effort of will or self-control is tiring: if you have had to force yourself to do something, you are less willing or less able to exert self-control when the next challenge comes around.
One of the main function of System 2 is to monitor and control thoughts and actions "suggested" by System 1, allowing some to be expressed directly in behavior and suppressing or modifying others
Cognition is embodied; you think with your body, not only with your brain
Common gesture can also unconsciously influence our thoughts and feelings
Evidently, a purely symbolic reminder of being watched prodded people into improved behavior; the effect occurs without any awareness

The consequences of repeated exposures benefit the organism in its relations to the immediate animate and inanimate environment. They allow the organism to distinguish objects and habitats that are safe from those that are not, and they are most primitive basis for social organization and cohesion - the basic sources of psychological and social stability.
Mood evidently affects the operation of System 1: when we are uncomfortable and unhappy, we lose touch with our intuition.
Sequence matters because the halo effect increase the weight of first impressions, sometimes to the point that subsequent information is mostly wasted.
The availability heuristic is a mental shortcut that relies on immediate examples that come to a given person's mind when evaluating a specific topic, concept, method or decision

The availability cascade is a self-reinforcing cycle that explains the development of certain kinds of collective beliefs. A novel idea or insight, usually one that seems to explain a complex process in a simple or straightforward manner, gains rapid currency in the popular discourse by its very simplicity and by its apparent insightfulness. Its rising popularity triggers a chain reaction within the social network.
The representativeness heuristic is used when making judgments about the probability of an event under uncertainty.
Probability judgments are higher for the richer and more detailed scenario, contrary to logic

Hindsight Bias, also known as the knew-it-all-along effect or creeping determinism, is the inclination, after an event has occurred, to see an event as having been predictable, despite there having been little or no objective basis for predicting it.

The first lesson is that errors of prediction are inevitable because the world is unpredictable. The  second is that high subjective confidence is not to be trusted as an indicator of accurancy.
Short term trend can be forecast, and behavior and achievements can be predicted with fair accuracy from previous behaviors and achievements
The Endowment Effect is the hypothesis that people ascribe more value to things merely because they own them

Loss Aversion in economics and decision theory refers to people's tendency to strongly prefer avoiding losses to acquiring gains. Most studies suggest that losses are twice powerful, psychologically, as gains.
Time is neglected, causing experiences that will retain their attention value in the long term to be appreciated less than they deserve to be.
nozioni:
**Commento Personale**
Molto interessante come faccia capire il vero modo che il nostro cervello ha di interpretare e reagire a quello che ci accade in ogni istante della nostra vita; di come non ci rendiamo conto di giudizi e impressioni che abbiamo e di come queste siano condizionate da singoli fattori che fanno variare completamente il pensiero che abbiamo di qualsiasi cosa.

Ho trovato il libro molto istruttivo e illuminante, ma a tratti troppo ricco di esempi un pò ripetitivi che rendevano a tratti la lettura noiosa e portavano alla voglia di saltare alcune pagine

title: L'uomo che scambiò suo moglie per un cappello
author: Oliver Sacks
quotes: Gli animali si ammalano, ma solo l'uomo cade radicalmente in preda alla malattia
Lo studio della malattia non può essere disgiunto da quello dell'identità

Si deve incominciare a perdere la memoria, anche solo brandelli di ricordi, per capire che in essa consiste la nostra vita. Senza memoria la vita non è vita. La nostra memoria è la nostra coerenza, nostra ragione, no sto sentimento, persino il nostro agire. Senza di essa non siamo nulla
concepts: Una malattia non è mai semplicemente una perdita o un eccesso, che c'è sempre una reazione, da parte dell'organismo o dell'individuo colpito, volta a ristabilire, a sostituire, a compensare e a conservare la propria identità, per strani che possono essere i mezzi usati.
nozioni:
**Commento Personale**
Libro molto scorrevole e piacevole, ma poco adatto a chi non fortemente interessato all'argomento.

Fondato su un lungo elenco di episodi con pazienti dell'autore che, anche se interessanti, tendono a stufare nell'arco della lettura del libro.

title: Pebbles of Perception: How a Few Good Choices Make All The Difference
author: Laurence Endersen
quotes: You can't put a wise head on young shoulders; but can we not be a little more wise, a little earlier?
Too soon old, too late wise
I keep six honest serving-men (They taught me all I Knew); Their names are What and When and How and Where and Who
ABC —> Always Be Curious
Listening: we have two ears and one mouth for a reason

There is no such thing as the view from nowhere, or from everywhere for that matter. Our point of view biases our observation, consciously ands unconsciously. You Cannot understand the view without the point of view.
When we understand the value of acceptance, stress subsides. Acceptance is pragmatic. We can't change what has already happened, but we can choose our reaction.
Introverts are as sociable as extroverts, they just prefer to be sociable in smaller groups. Being loud and abrasive is not sociable. It is the opposite.
Fear is mostly a thought-based construct. It dissolves when we drop our comparison and accept ourselves fully as we are, right now. Loss is a natural as birth. Choose real life over regret
Know yourself and see the journey. Be yourself and enjoy the journey. Mind yourself and extend the journey
Complexity is failed simplicity
Facts fill the mind; experiences open it. When you get opportunity to experience something different, take it. Differentiate yourself through your diversity of experiences.
concepts: First-level thinking might lead you to conclude that you are smart and in control. Second-level thinking suggest that there may be a large element of luck involved and that you need to keep your ego in check
To understand the nature of a live well lived, it is helpful first to consider the nature of a life that has been wasted.

Start by considering the end. Visualize both the road to personal fulfilment and the destination. Consider what behaviour would thwart that fulfilment and do the opposite. Thinking about the route to avoid helps reveal the more rewarding road.

Adversity does not discriminate. We can't avoid it - all we can do is seek to understand it and when we ultimately meet it recognise the question it is asking of us. The answer to that question is within us and no one else.
Our ability to choose is one of life's great gifts. We are the product of our choices. Good choices come from good character, and a few good choices make all the difference
For enthusiasm to endure, the purpose needs to be principled, that is, be grounded in wholesome motivations.

Our brains are powerful but far from perfect. While our gut, intuition and subconscious serve us well, for important decisions we need to think twice. Understanding our brains operate improves our ability to use them.
nozioni:
**Commento Personale**
Lettura davvero piacevole e ricca di spunti interessanti.
Libro che non svela segreti, ma che ricorda e spiega in modo semplice e chiaro quelli che sono i piccoli segreti per cercare di ottenere una vita migliore, oggi e nel futuro, sotto tutti gli aspetti, emozionali e economici

title: How to Read a Book
author: Charles Van Doren
quotes: Any book that can be read for understanding or information can probably be read for entertainment as well, just as a book that is capable of increasing our understanding can also be read purely for the information it contains.

What you understand by reading the book through to the end - even if it is only fifty percent or less - will help you when you make the additional effort later to go back to the places you passed by on your first reading. And even if you never go back, understanding half of a really tough book is much better than not understanding it at all, which will be the case if you even not start to read it.
Every book should be read no More slowly than it deserve, and numero quickly than can read it with satisfaction and comprehension.
You have to discover the meaning of a word you don’t understand by using the meaning of all the other words in the context that you do understand.
Reading a book is a kind of conversation
concepts: Enlightenment is achieved only when, in addition to knowing what an author says, you know what he means and why he says it.

There are 4 levels of reading:
- **Elementary Reading** - in mastering this level, one learns the rudiments of the art of reading, receives basic training in reading, and acquires initial reading skills.
- **Inspectional Reading** - it is characterized by its special emphasis on time. The aim is to examine the surface of the book, to learn everything that the surface alone can teach.
**Skimming** or **pre-reading** the book is the first sublevel of inspectional reading. The main aim is to discover whether the book requires a more careful reading; secondly, skimming can tell lots of other things about the book.
To do so, you can start looking at the title page and its preface, then having a look at the table of contents, then checking the index; more than that you can read the publisher’s blurb. One of the most common method to skim a book is to look at the chapters that seem to be pivotal to its argument; finally, turn the pages, dipping in here and there, reading a paragraph or two, sometimes several pages in sequence, never more than that.
**Superficial Reading**: In tackling a difficult book for the first time, read it through without ever stopping to look up or ponder the things you do not understand right away.
- **Analytical Reading** - it is thorough reading, complete reading, or good reading. It is the best and most complete reading that is possible given unlimited time.
There are 4 rules: 1- State what kind of book is the book you’re reading; 2- State the unity of the whole book in a single sentence; 3- Enumerate its major parts in their order and relation, and outline these parts as you have outlined the whole; 4- Define the problem or problems the author is trying to solve.
There is another rule, this is the rule 5- Find the important words and through them come to terms with the authors.
- **Syntopical Reading** - it is the most complex, active and systematical type of reading of all. It makes very heavy demands on the reader. It is the most rewarding of all reading activities.
-
nozioni:
**Commento Personale**
Inizialmente l’ho trovato interessante e con concetti piacevoli da seguire, con conseguente lettura piuttosto scorrevole; mano a mano che proseguivo nella lettura invece, questa è diventata sempre più pesante e noiosa, portandomi a perdere mano a mano il piacere nella lettura e la perdita di interesse per gli argomenti, che si facevano sempre più complessi.
Anche in questo caso il tutto è probabilmente enfatizzato dalle difficoltà dovute alla lingua inglese.

title: Diventa Chi Sei
author: Emilie Wapnick
quotes: Le scelte fanno paura, ma non sono quasi mai permanenti e non ci limitano mai davvero quanto pensiamo
Sopravvalutiamo quello che possiamo fare in un giorno, ma sottovalutiamo quello che possiamo fare in un anno
concepts: Abbraccio di Gruppo: permette di soddisfare tutti i bisogni di denaro, significato e varietà in un’unica carriera. Quindi in un solo lavoro o attività poliedrica il multipotenziale può incrociare diversi interessi nello stesso tempo

Alternanza: permette di esprimere con frequenza e facilità le varie sfaccettature dei multipotenziali; è una buona opzione per chi ama destreggiarsi con più progetti allo stesso tempo.
Quindi l’alternanza si riassume nell’avere 2 o più lavori e/o attività part-time che si alternano a ritmi regolari

Approccio Einstein: è un’ottima opzione per i multipotenziali che sono disposti a dedicarsi ai propri interessi per puro svago e, comunque, allo proprie condizioni. si addice a chi dà il meglio in condizioni di stabilità e routine. 
Questo approccio prevede quindi un solo lavoro o attività a tempo pieno che permetta di mantenersi a livello economico, lasciando abbastanza tempo ed energie per portare avanti in parallelo le altre passioni.

La Fenice: è l’opzione migliore per i multipotenziali che amano andare a fondo nelle cose e non hanno bisogno di una gran varietà per essere felici. È un approccio che consente di bilanciare il desiderio di approfondire e immergersi in un determinato settore coni il bisogno di fare esperienze diverse.
Adottare questo approccio significa lavorare in un solo ambito per più mesi o anni e poi cambiare marcia dando inizio a una nuova carriera.
nozioni:
**Commento Personale**
Seconda lettura di questo libro, letto la prima volta in lingua originale.
Confermo e alzo il giudizio iniziale per la facilità di lettura e scorrevolezza e per i concetti semplici ma, in qualche modo, illuminanti e utili nel riconoscere quello che sono davvero e il mio modo di affrontare le cose

title: Bella La Borsa, Peccato Quando Scende
author: Alberto Gaziano
quotes: Tutti possiedono le capacità mentali necessarie per fare soldi in Borsa. Ma non tutti hanno voglia e fegato sufficienti - Peter Lynch
Se ricerchi serenità resta pure sulla spiaggia, ma se ricerchi il tesoro devi correre il irschio di tuffarti in fondo all’oceano
La Borsa è un modo oneroso di scoprire il proprio temperamento - Adam Smith
Posso calcolare i movimenti dei corpi celesti, ma non riesco a farlo con la follia della gente - Isaac Newton
La migliore azione da acquistare potrebbe essere quella che possiedi già in portafoglio - Peter Lynch
Un mercato toro fa diventare competenti anche gli incompetenti
Un banchiere è uno che vi presta l’ombrello quando c’è il sole e lo rivuole indietro appena incomincia a piovere - Mark Twain
Seguire l’esperienza può farti sbagliare di tanto in tanto. Ma a non seguirla saresti veramente un asino - Jesse Livermore

I broker non sono amici. Sono come i dottori, pagati dai pazienti per cambiare le loro medicine. E sono pagati molto di più per i farmaci in promozione che per quelli che fanno bene al paziente - Warren Buffet
concepts: Quando la Borsa scende si può avere la tentazione di liquidare i propri investimenti e di aspettare finchè le condizioni non migliorino nuovamente. In teoria, questa sorta di “market timing” appare un piano allettante. Ma in pratica si potrebbero non ottenere i risultati sperati. È impossibile prevedere con precisione quali saranno le fasi successive che i mercati affronteranno. Anche quando i mercato incominciano a recuperare, probabilmente in base alle previsione di alcuni esperti, il recupero avrà vita breve. Per un corretto “market timing” è necessario prendere 2 decisioni: il momento migliore per acquistare e il momento migliore per vendere, ed è questo a complicare il tutto...”
Regola “Buffett”: Investite solo i soldi che non vi serviranno nei prossimi 20 anni e tenetevi pronti a discese anche superiori al 50%. Altrimenti non frequentate le Borse. Punto.

3 domande da porsi prima di decidere id acquistare azioni di una small cap:
1. La società ha una storia interessante e credibile?
2. C’è da fidarsi del management e della proprietà? Quello che nel passato ha promesso, l’ha poi mantenuto?
3. Si sarebbe disposti a comprare questo titolo anche prima della partenza di un lungo viaggio intorno al mondo senza la possibilità quindi di poter modificare per diverso tempo la propria posizione e opinione?
nozioni:
**Commento Personale**
Cosi come nelle mail settimanali che leggo con piacere, in questo libro (un pò datato) Salvatore spiega in tutte le salse e in modo molto chiaro quelli che sono le evidenze (ignorate da quasi tutti) degli investimenti in borsa per ottenere risultati nel lungo periodo.
Libro molto piacevole e facile da leggere anche per i meno esperti dell’argomento

title: Anything You Want: 40 Lessons for a New Kind of Entrepreneur
author: Derek Silvers
quotes: When you present an idea to the world and it’s not a hit, don’t keep pushing it as is. Instead, get back to improving and inventing
No business plan survives first contact with customers

Never forget that absolutely everything you do is for your customers. Make every decision - even decisions about whether to expand the business, raise money, or promote someone - according to what’s best for your customers

When you are thinking of how to make your business bugger, it’s tempting to try to think all the big thoughts and come up with world-changing massive-action plans. But please know that it’s often the tiny details that really thrill people enough to make them tell all their friends about you.

It’s about what you want to be, not what you want to have. To have something is the means, not the end. To be something is the real point. When you sign up to run a marathon, you don’t want a taxi to take to the finish line
A business is a reflection of the creator
concepts: Business in not about money. It’s about making dreams come true for others and for yourself
Making a company is a great way to improve the world while improving yourself
When you make a company, you make a utopia. It’s where you design your perfect world
Never do anything just for the money
Don’t pursue business just for your own gain. Only answer the calls for help
Success comes from persistently improving and inventing, not from persistently promoting what’s not working
Your business plan is moot. You don’t know what people really want until you start doing it
Starting with no money is an advantage. You don’t need money to start helping people
You can’t please everyone, so proudly exclude people
Make yourself unnecessary to the running of your business
The real point of doing anything is to be happy, so do only what makes you happy
nozioni:
**Commento Personale**
Libro di ispirazione per chiunque abbia aspirazione di creare un business proprio da zero. 
Lettura veloce e scorrevolissima, con tantissimi spunti utili da cui prendere ispirazione per cercare di partire con il piede giusto nel lancio di un’azienda

title: Fattore 1%: Piccole abitudini per grandi risultati
author: Luca Mazzucchelli
quotes: Alla base di ogni grande decisione e cambiamento della tua vita c’è un ‘emozione che ti ha spinto ad agire. E, a dire il vero, parlare di emozione è forse riduttivo, poichè le persone cambiano quando si trovano in mezzo a una vera e propria tempesta emotiva
La maggior parte di noi vive un a vita in bianco e nero. Le giornate sono fatte di lavoro, famiglia e sonno e noi siamo perlopiù fruitori passivi di quello che ci capita.
Più le nostre azioni sono coerenti con i nostri valori, più abbiamo la possibilità di condurre una vita felice
La vita è composta per il 20% da ciò che ci accade e per l’80% da come accogliamo quello che ci capita
Per cambiare gli altri bisogna innanzitutto cambiare sé stessi e il proprio atteggiamento mentale
Se vuoi di più, diventa di più
Per fare fiorire splendidamente un giardino, dobbiamo anche avere il coraggio di credere in quello che oggi non vediamo
In questo preciso momento noi siamo il risultato delle abitudini che abbiamo adottato negli ultimi 5 anni
concepts:
**Il Principio della rana bollita**: “Una rana salta per caso in una pentola appena messa su un fornello acceso. La fiamma è bassa e la rana nuota a suo agio in un’acqua pulita e appena tiepida, in un ambiente confortevole. L’acqua piano piano si riscalda, ma la rana la trova ancora piuttosto gradevole. La fiamma si alza ulteriormente e l’acqua ormai è diventata calda, un pò più di quanto la rana apprezzi. Ma non si spaventa, compie un piccolo sforzo e si abitua ben presto all’aumentare del calore. Mano a mano che l’acqua si scalda, la rana sente che la temperatura è davvero troppo alta e quindi decisamente sgradevole, purtroppo però, ormai si è indebolita e quindi non fa nulla per salvarsi. Cosi sopporta, sopporta, fino a quando la temperatura non si alza ancora, fino a ucciderla. 
Questo principio ci ricorda come, quando un cambiamento si realizza in maniera sufficientemente lenta e graduale, allora può sfuggire alla nostra coscienza, non suscitando alcun tipo di reazione e opposizione.
Il paradosso di questa storia è che se la rana si fosse immersa nella pentola nel momento in cui l’acqua bolliva, sarebbe subito saltata fuori o, per lo meno, ci avrebbe provato

Il paradosso della rana ci insegna che talvolta, senza rendercene conto, gradualmente rimaniamo incastrati proprio in ciò che ci fa male. Per evitare questo dobbiamo imparare ad essere vigili e a riflettere sugli effetti a lungo termine delle nostre scelte cosi da comprendere quando è l momento giusto per5 darsi la spinta, sempre prima che sia troppo tardi.
Le abitudini sono il nostro modo di reagire, appunto abitualmente, alle cose, senza pensarci.

Quali sono i valore che regolano quotidianamente la tua vita? Per capirlo poniti le seguenti 3 domande:
1. Cosa vorresti che la gente dicesse di te al tuo funerale?
2. Ti rimane un solo anno di vita. Come impieghi questo tempo?
3. Stai passeggiando per strada e ti crolla addosso un palazzo. Ti trovi imprigionato sotto le macerie e ti rendi conto di essere spacciato. Hai a disposizione solo 5 minuti di tempo per fare una telefonata. Chi chiami? Cosa gli dici?

Per aiutare a restare collegati quotidianamente con i propri valori, avendo consapevolezza di come quello che fai fornisce valore a te e agli altri, si può provare a tenere un diario in cui per 2 settimane, quotidianamente, andare a rispondere a queste 3 domande:
1. Quali sono i miei valori personali più importanti?
2. In che modo le mie azioni quotidiane si allineano con questi valori?
3. Come le mie azioni quotidiane possono faer la differenza per le altre persone?

**Legge di Pareto 80/20** - In molti casi, l’80% dei risultati è dato dal 20% degli sforzi. Per individuare quel 20% nella propria vita ci può porre queste 2 domande
1. Immagina che, per un evento assolutamente imprevedibile, il dottore ti obblighi a lavorare solo due ore al giorno e a riposare per tutte le altre. Cosa faresti in queste due ore di lavoro? Cosa tralasceresti?
2. Lo stesso medico ti richiama e ti dice che è necessario correggere ulteriormente la tua tabella di lavoro: puoi lavorare solo due ore alla settimana. Cosa faresti? Cosa taglieresti?

Affinchè un comportamento posa venire agilmente ripetuto nel tempo e diventi quindi un’abitudine, è necessario che possieda contemporaneamente queste 3 caratteristiche:
1. Deve essere attivabile con poco sforzo
2. Deve essere raggiungibile
3. Deve essere coinvolgente

Mindfulness è vivere nel momento presente in modo consapevole. Significa scegliere di risolvere tutta la propria attenzione a ciò che sta succedendo nel qui e ora con un atteggiamento curioso, aperto, non giudicante

L’ambiente che ci circonda può fornire molteplici soluzioni che vengono in soccorso alla nostra forza di volontà. Si tratta di cercarle, testarle e adottarle. Se non impariamo a organizzare l’ambiente in cui ci muoviamo in modo funzionale alle nostre scelte, saranno gli altri a scegliere per noi.
Il punto di vista di personae che provengono da settori diversi e utilizzano modelli mentali diversi dai propri, ma non per questo meno efficaci, comporta un grande arricchimento.

title: The Elephant in the Brain: Hidden Motives in Everyday Life
author: Kevin Simler & Robin Hanson
quotes: Every man alone is sincere. At the entrance of a second persone, hypocrisy begins - Ralph Waldo Emerson
The human brain was designed to deceive itself the better to deceive others
Often a species’ most important competitor is itself
A man always has two reason for doing anything” a good reason and the real reason - J.P. Morgan
The man who reads nothing at all is better educated than the man who reads nothing but newspapers - Thomas Jefferson
concepts:
We, human-beings, are a species that’s not only capable of acting on hidden motives - we’re designed to do it. Our brains are built to act in our self-interest while at the same time trying hard not to appear selfish in front of other people. And in order to throw them off the trail, our brains often keep “us”, our conscious minds, in the dark. The less we know of our own ugly motives, the easier it to hide them from others.
Self-deception is therefore strategic, a ploy our brains use to look good while behaving badly
We are strategically ignorant about our own motives. In other words, we don’t always know the “whys” behind our own behavior, but we certainly pretend to know
The Halo Effect - Is the tendency for positive impressions of a persone, company, brand or product in one area to positively influence one’s opinion or feeling in other areas.
We have many reason for our behaviors, but we habitually accentuate and exaggerate our pretty, prosocial motives and downplay our ugly, selfish ones.
In a conversation, participants evaluate each other not just as trading partners, but also as potential allies.

By attending a sermon, you’re learning not just what “God” or the preacher thinks, but also what the rest of your congregation is willing to accept
-

author: Dale Carnegie
quotes: Any fool che criticise, condemn and complain - and most fools do. But it takes character and self-control to be understanding and forgining.
To know all is to forgive all.
Use what language you will, you can never say anything but what you are.
If there is any one secret of success, it lies in the ability to get the other person’s point of view and see things from that person’s angle as well as from your own.
We are interested in others when they are interested in us.
You cannot teach a man anything; you can only help him to find it within himself.
I have no right to say or do anything that diminishes a man in his own eyes. What matters is noto what I think of him, but what he thinks of himself. Hurting a man in his dignity is a crime.
concepts: Criticism is futile because it puts a persone on the defensive and usually makes him strive to justify himself. Criticism is dangerous, because it wounds a person’s precious pride, hurts his sense of importance, and arouses resentment.
When dialing with people, let us remember we are not dealing with creatures of logic. We are dealing with creatures of emotion, creatures bristling with prejudices and motivated by pride and vanity

Instead of condemning people. let’s try to understand them. Let’s try to figure out why they do what they do. That’s a lot more profitable and intriguing than criticism; and it breeds sympathy, tolerance and kindness

### PRINCIPLE 2

title: The Internet of Money (Vol. 1)
author: Andreas Antonopoulos
concepts: Bitcoin is the first network-centric protocol-based form of money. That means it exists without reference to an institutional or platform context.

Disruptarian: what that means is that every 30 or 40 years at least, things that have settled need to be disrupted. Because as they settle, power accumulates, they become centralized, and with centralized power, corruption happens.

Bitcoin isn't a digital currency. It's a cryptocurrency. It's a network-centric money.

title: The Internet of Money (Vol. 2)
author: Andreas Antonopoulos
quotes: The essence of bitcoin is the ability to operate in a decentralized way without having to trust anyone.
Bitcoin is noto trying to become a national currency. Oh no, it’s doing something far more dangerous. It’s encouraging people to put their savings outside the system.
concepts: Bitcoin introduces a platform on which you can run currency as an application, on a network without any central points of control, a system completely decentralized like the internet itself. It is not money for the internet, but rather the Internet of Money

Bitcoin unifies system of money. Today we have system of money from small payments, systems of money for large payments. We have systems of money for payments between individuals, for payments between companies, and for payments between governments.
Does that remind you of something? That’s how communication used to be before the internet. We had systems of communication for pictures, system of communication for letters, ecc. The internet came along and unified all of those.
Blockchain is one of the four foundational technologies (Blockchain, Proof-of-Work, P2P Network, and Cryptography) behind bitcoin, and it can’t stand alone.
The whole point of a decentralized blockchain is that you don’t keep the in formation secure; you spread it out so thin there’s no place to attack it directly. That’s what makes it secure.

There are three elements to success in this industry:
1. Identifying a viable market
2. The right timing
3. Sequencing - you can’t build a system of complexity that depends on many-to-many interaction with high density when the market is still focused on delivering applications that are one-to-one with low density
The characteristic that gives bitcoin its tamper-proof capability is not “the blockchain”; it’s proof-of-work. Proof-of-work is what makes bitcoin fundamentally immutable.

Mining doesn’t work to create bitcoin. That is not the purpose of mining; that is the side effect. Mining will continue to exist because its purpose is to provide security, to provide validation of all the transactions and blocks according to the consensus rules.

La forza di bitcoin è data anche dalla sua resilienza; negli anni è stato in grado di resistere a ogni tipo di attacco esterno, andando ogni volta a rinforzare il suo network. Cosi come l’essere umano che quando viene esposto ad agenti esterni sviluppa le difese contro lo stesso e si rafforza.
The fundamental characteristic of the new money is decentralization and choice. Bitcoin does not compete against other currencies it creates a new own environment.

Con Lightning Network è possibile creare e ricevere transazioni in modo immediato, con zero commissioni, garantendo privacy e anonimato. È un sistema di smart contract, dove non è necessario avere fiducia in nessuno dei partecipanti al sistema. Si basa su un semplice sistema di input e output, ma sarà la rete e il sistema stesso a combinare le varie transazioni così da fare in modo che, solo al momento in cui tutti i partecipanti al sistema avranno fatto tutte le transazioni necessarie, verrà inviata un’unica transazione complessiva alla Blockchain Bitcoin.

We are going to open up a completely new dimension to money, one that allows us to address money as minute flows that flow continuously, that can be aggregated and split as streams.

author: Andreas Antonopoulos
concepts: We have this wonderful thing called fractional reserve banking. If you are unfamiliar with fractional reserve banking, the gist of it is that commercial banks take one dollar and create another nine dollars out of thin air. If you explained that to a five-year-old, they would probably turn around and say “that sounds like fraud!”. They would be right.

Every time you use crypto, you are exercising sovereign power that is diverting money from a corrupt and broken system of central banks, investment banks, and governments, into a system that is giving people hope. Every time you use crypto, you are helping to fund the projects that make wallets and decentralized exchanges better; you are helping to fund new innovation and research. How many people here are developers who work in Bitcoin and other open blockchains? The rest of you are paying these people to do better work. Every time you use crypto, you are exercising power.

A classic trilemma is: security, decentralization and scalability. In a trilemma, you can only pick two of the three options. If you make something that is maximally scalable and decentralized, it probably won’t be secure.

title: Principles: Life and Work
author: Ray Dalio
quotes: Time is like a river that carries us forward into encounters with reality that require us to make decisions. We can’t stop our movement down this river and we can’t avoid those encounters. We can only approach them in the best possible way.

If you are ready to give up everything else and study the whole history and background of the market and all principal companies whose stocks are on the board as carefully as a medical studies anatomy - if you can do all that and in addition you have tho cool nerves of a gambler, the sixth sense of a clairvoyant and the courage of a lion, you have a ghost of a chance to make money in the markets - Bernard Baruch
He who lives by the crystal ball is destined to eat ground glass
There is no worse course in leadership than to hold out false hopes soon to be swept away - Winston Churchill
You have to have a willingness to repeatedly fail. If you don’t have a willingness to fail, you’re going to have to be very careful not to invent” - Jeff Bezos
When you know what someone is like, you know what you can expect from them
concepts: Audacious goals —> Failure —> Learning Principles —> Improving —> More audacious goals
We are all born with different thinking abilities but we aren’t born with decision-making skills. We learn them from our encounters with reality.

You better make sense of what happened to other people in other times and other places because if you don’t, you won’t know if these things can happen to you and, if they do, you won’t know hot to deal with them.

To do exceptionally well you have to push yours limits and that, if you push your limits, you will crash and it will hurt a lot. You will think you have failed - but that won’t be true unless you give up. Believe it or not, your pain will fade and you will have many other opportunities ahead of you, though you might not see them at the time. The most important thing you can do is to gather the lessons these failures provide and gain humility and radical open-mindedness in order to increase your chances of success. Then you press on.

Be curious enough to what to understand how the people who see things differently from you came to see them that way. You will find that interesting and invaluable, and the richer perspective you gain will help you decide what you should do.
Making a handful of good uncorrelated bets that are balanced and leveraged well is the surest way of having a lot of upside without being exposed to unacceptable downside.
Look to the patterns of those things that affect you in order to understand the cause-effect relationships that drive them and to learn principles for dealing with them effectively.
An organization is a machine consisting of two major parts: culture and people.

We work with others to get three things:
- leverage to accomplish our chosen mission in bigger and better ways than we could alone.
- Quality relationship that together make for a great community.
- Money that allows us to buy what we need and want for ourselves and others.
nozioni:
**Life Principles**
**Embrace Reality and Deal with it**
1. Be a hyperrealistic
Being hyperrealistic will help you choose your dreams wisely and then achieve them.
2. Truth - or more precisely, an accurate understanding of reality - is the essential foundation for any good outcome.
3. Be radically open-minded and radically transparent.
The more open-minded you are, the less likely you are to deceive yourself, and more likely it is that others will give you honest feedback.
Don’t let fears of what others think of you stand in your way.
4. Look to nature to learn how reality works.
Don’t get hung up on your views of how things “should” be because you will miss out on learning how they really are.
the key is to fail, lear, and improve quickly. If you are constantly learning and improving, your evolutionary process will look like the one that’s ascending.
5. Evolving is life’s greatest accomplishment and its greatest reward.
6. Understand nature’s practical lessons.
7. Pain + Reflection = Progress.
If you can develop a reflexive reaction to physic pain that causes you to reflect on it rather than avoid it, it will lead to your rapid learning/evolving.
The challenges you face will test and strengthen you. If you’re not failing, you’re not pushing your limits, and if you’re not pushing your limits, you’re not maximizing your potential. Though this process of pushing your limits , of sometimes failing and sometimes breaking through - and delivering benefits from both your failures and your successes - is not for everyone, if it is you, it can be so thrilling that it becomes addictive. Life will inevitably bring you such moments, and it’ll be up to you decide whether you want to go back for more.
8. Weigh second - and third - order consequences.
9. Own your outcomes.
10. Look at the machine from the higher level.
You shouldn’t be upset if you find out that you’re bad at something - you should be happy that you found out, because knowing that and dealing with it will improve your chances of getting what you want.

**Use the 5-Step Process to Get What You Want Out of Life**
1. Have clear goals.
2. Identify and don’t tolerate the problems that stand in the way of your achieving those goals.
3. Accurately diagnose the problems to get at their root causes.
4. Design plans that will get you around them.
5. Do what’s necessary to push these designs through to results.

**Be Radically Open-Minded**
1. Recognize your two barriers.
The two big barriers to good decision making are your ego and your blind spots.
The ego barrier is your subliminal defense mechanism that make it hard for you to accept your mistake and weakness.
2. Practice radical open-mindedness.
3. Appreciate the art of thoughtful disagreement.
4. Triangulate your view with believable people who are willing to disagree.
5. Recognize the signs of closed-mindedness and open-mindedness that you should watch out of you.
Open-minded people are always interested in listening than in speaking; they encourage others to voice their views.
Open-minded people approach everything with a deep-seated fear that they may be wrong.
Open-minded people are more curious about why there is disagreement.
Open-minded people genuinely believe they could be wrong; the questions that they ask are genuine.
Open-minded people always feel compelled to see things through others’ eyes.
6. Understand how you can become radically open-minded.
Becoming truly open-minded takes time. Like all real learning, doing this is largely a matter of habit; once you do it so many times it is almost instinctive, you’ll find it intolerable to be any other way. This typically takes about eighteen months, which in the course of a lifetime is nothing.

**Understand That People Are Wired Very Differently**
1. Understanding the power that comes from knowing how you and others are wired.
Everyone is like a Lego set of attributes, with each piece reflecting the workings of a different part of the brain. All these pieces come together to determine what each person is like, and if you know what a person is like, you’ll have a pretty idea of what you can expect from them.
2. Meaningful work and meaningful relationships aren’t just nice things we chose for ourselves - they are genetically programmed into us.
3. Understanding the great brain battles and how to control them to get what you want.
4. Find out what you and the others are like.
5. Getting the right people in the right roles in support of your goal is the key to succeeding at whatever you choose to accomplish.

**Learn How to Make Decisions Effectively**
Thinking —> Principles —> Algorithms —> Great Decision
1. Recognize that 1) the biggest threat to good decision making is harmful emotions, and 2) decision making is a two-step process (first learning and then deciding).
2. Synthesize the situation at hand.
One of the most important decision you can make is who you ask questions of.
Listening to uninformed people is worse than having no answers at all.
3. Synthesize the situation through time.
4. Navigate levels effectively.
5. Logic, reason, and common sense are your best tools for synthesizing reality and understanding what to do about it.
6. Make your decisions as expected value calculations.
7. Prioritize by weighing the value of additional information against the cost of not deciding.
8. Simplify.
9. Use principles.
10. Believability weight your decision making.
11. Convert your principles into algorithms and have the computer make decisions alongside you.
12. Be cautious about trusting AI without having deep understanding.

**Work Principles**
**Trust in Radical Truth and Radical Transparency**
Being radically truthful and transparent with your colleagues and expecting your colleagues to be the same with you ensures that important issues are apparent instead of hidden; it also enforces good behavior and good thinking. Radical truth and radica transparency are fundamental to having a real idea meritocracy.
1. Realize that you have nothing to fear from knowing the truth.
2. Have integrity and demand it from others.
3. Create an environment in which everyone has the right to understand what makes sense and no one has the right to hold a critical opinion without speaking up.
Be extremely open. Discuss your issues until you are in sync with each other or until you understand each other's positions and can determine what should be done. As someone I worked with once explained "It's simple, just don't filter"
4. Be radically transparent.
5. Meaningful relationships and meaningful work are mutually reinforcing, especially when supported by radical truth and radical transparency.

**Cultivate Meaningful Work and Meaningful Relationships**
1. Be loyal to the common mission and not to anyone who is not operating consistently with it.
2. Be crystal clear on what the deal is.
3. Recognize that the size of the organization can pose a threat to meaningful relationship.
4. Remember that the most people will pretend to operate in your interest while operating in their own.
5. Treasure honorable people who are capable and will treat you well even when you’re not looking.

**Create a Culture in Which It Is Okay to Make Mistakes and Unacceptable Not to Learn from Them**
In Bridgewater we work together to build an error log (we now call it the Issue Log), in which traders recorded all their mistakes and bad outcomes so we could track them and address them systematically. Our environment is one in which people understand that remarks such as “You handled that badly” are meant to be helpful rather than punitive.
1. Recognize that mistakes are a natural part of the evolutionary process.
2. Don’t worry about looking good - worry about achieving your goals.
Remember that what was already happened lies in the past and no longer matters except as a lesson for the future. The need for phony praise needs to be unlearned.
3. Observe the patterns of mistakes to see if they are products of weakness.
4. Remember to reflect when you experience pain.
Because these moments of pain are so important, you shouldn’t rush through them. Stay in them and explore them so you can build a foundation for improvement.
Pain + Reflection = Progress
5. Know what types of mistakes are acceptable and what types are unacceptable, and don’t allow the people who work for you to make the unacceptable ones.

**Get and Stay in Sync**
1. Recognize that conflicts are essential for great relationships because they are how people determine whether their principles are aligned and resolve their differences.
Spend lavishly on the time and energy you devote to getting in sync, because it’s the best investment you can make.
2. Know how to get in sync and disagree well.
3. Be open-minded and assertive at the same time.
Open-minded people seek to learn by asking questions; they realize how little they know in relation to what there is to know and recognize that they might be wrong; they are thrilled to be around people who know more then they do because it represents an opportunity to learn something.
In any conversation, there is a responsibility to express and a responsibility to listen. The parties involved should always consider the possibility that one or both oof them misunderstood and go back-and-forth so that they can get in sync.
4. If it is your meeting to run, manage the conversation.
Make it clear who is directing the meeting and whom it is meant to serve. Meeting without someone clearly responsible runa high risk of being directionless and unproductive.
Be precise in what you are talking about to avoid confusion,
Make clear what type of communication you are going to have in light of the objectives and priorities. If your goal is to have people with different opinions work through their differences to try to get closer to what is true and what to do about ii, you will run your meeting differently than if its goal is to educate.
Lead the discussion by being assertive and open-minded.
5. Great collaboration feels like playing jazz.
In jazz, there is no script: you have to figure things out as you go along. Sometimes you need to sit back and let others drive things; other times, you blare it out yourself. To do the right thing at the right moment you need to really listen to the people you’re playing with so that you can understand where they’re going. All great creative collaboration should feel the same way.
Two people who collaborate well will be about three times as effective as each of them operating independently.
6. When you have alignment, cherish it.
7. If you find you can’t reconcile major differences - especially in values - consider whether the relationship is worth preserving.
If you find you can’t get in sync with someone on shared values, you should consider whether that person is worth keeping in your life. A lack of common values will lead to a lot of pain and other harmful consequences and may ultimately drive you apart.

**Believability Weight Your Decision Making**
1. Recognize that having an effective idea meritocracy requires that you understand the merit of each person’s idea.
2. Find the most believable people possible who disagree with you and try to understand their reasoning.
3. Think about whether you are playing the role of a teacher, a student, or a peer and whether you should be teaching, asking questions, or debating.
4. Understand how people came by their opinions.
5. Disagreeing must be done efficiently.
6. Recognize that everyone has the right and responsibility to try to make sense of important things.
7. Pay more attention to whether the decision-making system is fair than whether you get your way.

**Recognize Hot to Get Beyond Disagreement**
1. Remember: Principles can’t be ignored by mutual agreement.
2. Make sure people don’t confuse the right to complain, give  advice. and openly debate with the right to make decisions.
3. Don’ leave important conflicts unresolved.
4. Once a decision is made, everyone should get behind it even though individuals may still disagree.
A decision-making group in which those who don’t get what they want continue to fight rather than work for what the group has decided is destined to fail.
The group is more important than the individual; don’t behave in a way that undermines the chosen path.
5. Remember that if the idea meritocracy comes into conflict with the well-being of the organization, it will inevitably suffer.
6. Recognize that if the people who have the power don’t want to operate by principles, the principled way of operating will fail.

**Remember That the WHO is More Important than the WHAT**
1. Recognize that the most important decision for you to make is who you choose as your Responsible Parties.
2. Know that the ultimate Responsible Party will be the person who bears the consequences of what is done.
When putting someone in a position of responsibility, make sure their incentives are aligned with their responsibilities and they experience the consequences of the outcomes they produce.
Make sure that everyone has someone they report to.
3. Remember the force behind the thing.

**Hire Right, Because the Penalties for Hiring Wrong Are Huge**
1. Match the person to the design.
Think through which values, abilities, and skills you are looking for (in that order). Values are the deep-seated beliefs that motivate behaviors and determine people’s compatibilities with each other. People will fight for their values, and they are likely to fight with people who don’t share them. Abilities are ways of thinking and behaving. Skill are learned tools.
The process for choosing people should be systematically built out and evidence-based.
2. Remember that people are built very differently and that different ways of seeing and thinking make people suitable for different jobs.
Understand how to use and interpret personality assessments. Personality assessments are valuable tool for getting a quick picture of what people are like in terms of their abilities, preferences, and style. They are often more objective and reliable than interviews.
Remember that people tend to pick people like themselves, so choose interviewers who can identify what you are looking for.
Remember that people typically don’t change all that much. This is especially true over short periods of time like a year or two, yet most people want to assume that when someone does something wrong the person will learn the lesson and change. That’s naive. It is best to assume that they won’t change unless there is good evidence to the contrary that they will.
3. Think of your teams the way that sports managers do: No one person possesses everything required to produce success, yet everyone must excel.
4. Pay attention to people’s track records.
Recognize that performance in school doesn’t tell you much about whether a person has the values and abilities you are looking for.
5. Don’t hire people just to fit the first job they will do; hire people you want to share your life with.
Look for people who have lots of questions.
6. When considering compensation. provide both stability and opportunity.
7. Remember that in great partnerships, consideration and generosity are more important than money.
8. Great people are hard to find so make sure you think how to keep them.

**Constantly Train, Test, Evaluate, and Sort People**
Every Leader must decide between 1) getting rid of liked but incapable people to achieve their goals and 2) keeping the nice but incapable people and not achieving their goals. Whether or not you can make these hard decisions is the strongest determination of your own success or failure.
1. Understand that you and the people you manage will go through a process of personal evolution.
2. Provide constant feedback.
3. Evaluate accurately, not kindly.
Nobody ever said radical honesty was easy. Sometimes, especially with new employees who have not yet gotten used to it, an honest assessment feels like an attack. Rise to a higher level and keep your eye on the bigger picture and counsel the person you are evaluating to do the same.
4. Recognize that tough love is both the hardest and the most important type of love to give (because it is so rarely welcomed).
The greatest gift you can give someone is the power to be successful. Giving people the opportunity to struggle rather than giving them the things they are struggling for will make them stronger.
5. Don’t hide your observations about people.
6. Make the process of learning what someone is like open, evolutionary, and iterative.
Encourage people to be objectively reflective about their performance. Being able to see yourself from a higher level is essential for personal evolution and achieving your goals.
Remember that when it comes to assessing people, the two biggest mistakes you can make are being overconfident in your assessment and failing to get in sync on it.
7. Knowing how people operate and being able to judge whether that way of operating will lead to good results is more important than knowing what they did.
8. Recognize that when you are really in sync with someone about their weaknesses, the weaknesses are probably true.
9. Train, Guardrail, or remove people: don’t rehabilitate them.
10. Remember that the goal of a transfer is the best, highest use of the person in a way that benefits the community as a whole.
11. Don’t lower the bar.

**Manage as Someone Operating a Machine to Achieve a Goal**
1. Look down on your machine and yourself within it from the higher level.
No manager at any level can expect to succeed without the skill set of an organizational engineer.
2. Remember that for every case you deal with, your approach should have two purposes 1) to move you closer to your goal, and 2) to train and test your machine (your people and your design).
3. Understand the differences between managing, micromanaging and not managing.
4. Know what your people are like and what makes them tick, because your people are your most important resource.
5. Clearly assign responsibilities.
6. Probe deep and hard to learn what you can expect from your machine.
7. Think like an owner, and expect the people you work with to do the same.
8. Recognize and deal with key-man risk.
9. Don't treat everyone the same - treat them appropriately.
10. Know that great leadership is generally not what it's made out to be.
11. Hold yourself and your people accountable and appreciate them for holding you accountable.
12. Communicate the plan clearly and have clear metrics conveying whether you care progressing according to it.
13. Escalate when you can't adequately handle your responsibilities and make sure that the people who work for you are proactive about doing the same.

**Perceive and Don’t Tolerate Problems**
Every problem you find is an opportunity to improve your machine.
1. If you’re not worried, you need to worry - and if you’re worried, you don’t need to worry.
2. Design and oversee a machine to perceive whether things are good enough or not good enough, or do it yourself.
3. Be very specific about problems; don’t start with generalizations.
4. Don’t be afraid to fix the difficult things.

**Diagnose Problems to Get at Their Root Causes**
The most common mistake people make is dealing with their problems as one-offs rather than using them to diagnose how their machine is working so that they can improve it. They move on to fix problems without getting at their root causes, which is a recipe for continue failure. The second most common mistake people make is to depersonalize the diagnosis. Not connecting problems to the people who failed and not examining what it is about them that caused the failure will not lead to improvements of the individuals or the machine. The third biggest reason for failure is to not connect what one is learning in one diagnosis to what was learned in prior ones. It is important to determine whether the root cause of a particular problem is a part of a larger pattern or not.
1. To diagnose well, ask the following questions:
1. Is the outcome good or bad?
2. Who is responsible for the outcome?
3. If the outcome is bad, is the Responsible Party incapable and/or is the design bad?
2. Maintain an emerging synthesis by diagnosing continuously.
3. Keep in mind that diagnoses should produce outcomes.
4. Use the following “drill-down” technique to gain an 80/20 understanding of a department or sub-department that is having problems.
Step 1 - List the problems
Step 2 - Identify the Root Causes. To get at the root causes, keep asking “Why?”
Step 3 - Create a plan
Step 4 - Execute the plan
5. Understand that diagnosis is foundational to both progress and quality relationship

**Design Improvements to Your Machine to Get Around Your Problems**
1. Build your machine.
2. Systemize your principles and how they will be implemented.
3. Remember that a good plan should resemble a movie script.
4. Recognize that design is an iterative process. Between a bad “now” and a good “then” is a “working through it” period.
5. Build the organization around goal rather than tasks.
6. Create an organization chart to look like a pyramid, with straight lines down that don’t cross.
7. Create guardrails when needed - and remember it’s better not to guardrail at all.
8. Keep your strategic vision the same while making appropriate tactical changes as circumstances dictate.
9. Have good controls so that you are not exposed to the dishonesty of others.
10. Have the clearest possible reporting lines and delineations of responsibilities.
11. Remember that almost everything will take more time and cost more money than you expect.

**Do What You Set Out to Do**
1. Work for goals that you and your organization are excited about and think about how your tasks connect to those goals.
Don’t act before thinking. Take the time to come up with a game plan. The time you spend on thinking through your plan will be virtually nothing in relation to the amount of time that will be spent doing, and it will make the doing radically more effective.
2. Recognize that everyone has too much to do.
3. Use checklists.
4. Allow time for rest and renovation.
5. Ring the bell.

**Use Tools and Protocols to Shape How Work Is Done**
1. Having systemized principles embedded in tools is especially valuable for an idea meritocracy.

**And for Heaven’s Sake, Don’t Overlook Governance!**
1. To be successful, all organizations must have checks and balances.
2. Remember that in an idea meritocracy a single CEO is not as good as a great group of leaders.
3. No governance system of principles, rules, and checks and balances can substitute for a great partnership.

**Commento Personale**
Ray Dalio ha una capacità di descrivere in modo davvero efficace e chiaro i concetti che vuole spiegare. Il libro risulta quindi molto scorrevole e piacevole.
I consigli e principi elencati dall’autore sono fonte di grandi stimoli e pensieri e potranno sicuramente risultare utili da applicare in un percorso imprenditoriale che vorrei intraprendere.

title: La Canzone di Achille
author: Madeline Miller
quotes: Diceva ciò che pensava e restava stupito quando gli altri non facevano lo stesso. Qualcuno avrebbe potuto scambiare quel tratto per ingenuità. Ma non è una caratteristica del genio andare sempre dritto al cuore?

Libro davvero intenso ed emozionante
